items.mistake: Using very deep trees as weak learners in boosting
items.fix: Keep max_depth small (often 3–8 for GBM, sometimes 1–3 for teaching); let many rounds compose complexity instead of one deep tree.
items.why: Deep trees in boosting overfit quickly because each round can memorize residual noise; shallow trees force incremental corrections.
items.example: max_depth=12 with 500 estimators may hit perfect training MSE but fail validation; max_depth=3 with the same budget often generalizes better.

items.mistake: Setting learning_rate=1.0 with hundreds of trees
items.fix: Pair a small η (0.01–0.1) with more n_estimators, or reduce n_estimators when η is large; tune both jointly on validation data.
items.why: η=1 adds each tree at full strength—later trees chase noise from earlier overshoot instead of smooth improvement.
items.example: η=0.05 and n_estimators=400 often beats η=0.3 and n_estimators=100 on the same validation curve budget.

items.mistake: Treating gradient boosting like random forest (parallel, independent trees)
items.fix: Remember trees are sequential; early stopping on validation loss and monitoring staged predictions reflect the corrective loop.
items.why: Misunderstanding the training order leads to wrong expectations about bagging benefits, feature subsampling, and why later trees matter.
items.example: Shuffling training rows between rounds does not create independent trees—the m-th tree explicitly targets residuals after trees 1..m−1.
